title: Quick check: When to break the rules

questions.prompt: Three checks before deviating from a default?

choices.text: (1) Have you tried the default cleanly — long enough to read real signal, not a half-hearted version? (2) Do you have data showing the default fails — hypothesis isn't data; run the test. (3) Can you articulate WHY the default fails for your specific case — not just restate that it does. Example articulated why: 'My audience is 200k decision-makers at SaaS companies; broad match would waste 99% of impressions on consumer users.' If all three check out, deviating is right; otherwise the default is your safest bet.

questions.explanation: Tried default, have data, can articulate why. All three required.

questions.prompt: B2B SaaS to 200k decision-makers at specific company types. Broad match and PMax failing. Justified deviation?

choices.text: Switch to narrow targeting (Customer Match list of target accounts + custom audiences from competitor URLs + in-market B2B SaaS), dedicated Search campaigns with phrase/exact match types and strong negatives, manual or cost-cap-style Smart Bidding (tCPA) to enforce predictable per-lead cost. The audience is structurally too small for broad match to be efficient; PMax's broad-by-design fights the offer. Three deviations stacked because the underlying account isn't median: narrow audience + dedicated channel + controlled bidding. Run for 4-6 weeks before judging.

questions.explanation: B2B with narrow audience justifies narrow + Search-only + tighter bidding.
